ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for Phuket Fish Curry specifically; FOODON_03311069 (fish curry) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (fish, cooked, in sauce) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Phuket fish curry code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Phuket fish curry or a sufficiently close fish curry concept suitable for clinical coding.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Phuket Fish Curry is approximately 300 g, comprising a substantial piece of fish (roughly 150 g) in a generous portion of spiced sauce, served alongside rice or naan.

nutrient_source.title: Fish curry, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked fish in tomato-based curry sauce for cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the closest approved primary source for an Indian fish curry. Values are adapted from the fish curry entry (white fish in spiced tomato-coconut sauce). The Phuket sauce element introduces Thai-style spicing but does not materially alter the macronutrient profile relative to a standard Indian fish curry. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 35196, fish curry) used for cross-validation of protein and fat values. Per-100 g values represent the combined dish (fish + sauce) as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish curry dishes — analogous estimate based on fish curry (GI ~35–40) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Phuket Fish Curry. GI is estimated at 38 based on analogous fish curry entries (GI range 35–40) in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The low GI reflects the high protein content of fish, moderate fat from coconut milk, and low net carbohydrate content of the sauce. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 4.3, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 3.8 × 3 = 11.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of Phuket Fish Curry may contribute approximately 960 mg sodium, representing roughly 40–48% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2400 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) and low net carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Phuket Fish Curry has a low glycemic index of approximately 38 and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.3), making it a suitable protein-rich, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~2.8 g per 100 g) and sodium load per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, the coconut milk component contributes saturated fat (~2.8 g per 100 g), and the sodium content per restaurant serving is elevated; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and prefer preparations with reduced coconut milk and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Phuket Fish Curry is an Indian restaurant dish served in Singapore that features a substantial piece of white fish — typically a firm-fleshed variety such as snapper, barramundi, or pomfret — simmered in a richly spiced sauce that blends Indian curry aromatics (turmeric, cumin, coriander, curry leaves, chilli, ginger, garlic, tomato) with a Thai-inspired Phuket-style element, often incorporating coconut milk and lemongrass for a mildly tangy, fragrant, and moderately spiced flavour profile. The dish is associated with Anjana Kitchen, an Indian restaurant in Singapore's financial district. The sauce is typically medium-thick, orange-red in colour, and aromatic. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (~12.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~6.2 g per 100 g, including saturated fat from coconut milk), and low in carbohydrates (~3.8 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 118 kcal per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 38 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate-to-high at ~320 mg per 100 g, which is clinically relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal. It is commonly served with rice or naan bread in a restaurant context.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Phuket Fish?
Is Phuket Fish Curry good for diabetics?
What fish is used in Phuket Fish Curry?
What is the sodium content of fish curry?
Indian fish curry with coconut milk calories

confidence_reason: No exact match for Phuket Fish Curry exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN fish curry entry (2017), which is the closest approved primary source for an Indian fish curry dish. The Thai-inspired Phuket sauce element does not materially alter the macronutrient profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN fish curry source entry used for this record. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a home-style cooked fish curry with no hydrogenated fats in the ingredient profile. 5. The input record listed cuisine as 'Indian' and cuisine_tags as Indian/Curry/Naan; the food_name 'Phuket Fish' has been expanded to 'Phuket Fish Curry' to reflect the canonical dish identity more precisely. 6. The dish is served by Anjana Kitchen - Six Battery Road, Singapore, at SGD 25.50. 7. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous fish curry entries; glycemic load per 300 g serving ≈ 4.3. 8. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore by an Indian restaurant. 9. Thai local name and aliases added to address validator feedback regarding 'Phuket' in the name and 'Thai-inspired' description, ensuring comprehensive multilingual coverage.
